skill.name: tdd-workflows-tdd-green
skill.description: Guide an AI coding assistant through the TDD green phase by making the smallest implementation change that passes failing tests.
skill.summary: Helps implement minimal passing code during the TDD green phase.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, sensitive data, cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found these are Markdown examples and ordinary test guidance rather than executable skill code. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, malicious network use, or bundled secret material was found. The remaining risk is low because the skill asks an AI assistant to edit code and run tests in the user workspace.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Examples Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are Markdown code fences and language examples. They are instructional samples, not executable skill scripts, so the static command findings are false positives.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are inside fenced TypeScript, JavaScript, Python, Django, React, and Express examples. No file contains runnable wrapper code that executes those snippets automatically.

low_findings.title: Local Test Example Misclassified as Network Access
low_findings.description: The network finding at the API test line uses a local request helper against an application object. It does not contact an external host or transmit data outside the workspace.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code is a Supertest-style local API test example using request(app). No URL, host, webhook, or outbound client configuration appears at the cited location.

low_findings.title: Database Examples Misclassified as Sensitive SQLite Files
low_findings.description: The sensitive data findings are example SQL queries and repository calls in a TDD playbook. They do not include database files, credentials, dumps, or real user data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text shows parameterized example queries and abstract database inserts. There is no SQLite file path, binary database content, or secret-bearing connection string.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Flags Lack Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged weak cryptography and reconnaissance terms, but the reviewed lines are TDD descriptions, examples, or common framework code. No hashing, encryption, host discovery, port scanning, or system inventory behavior is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found normal prose and test-framework syntax at the flagged lines. The static labels appear to come from substring matches rather than security-relevant behavior.

low_findings.title: Critical Heuristic Combination Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The critical static heuristic combined code examples, one local API test helper, and database sample text. Manual review found no credential access, no obfuscation, and no external data transfer.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited evidence is explainable as Markdown examples and local test code. I found no semantic evidence of credential collection, encoded payloads, or outbound exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Workspace Modification and Test Execution Require User Trust
low_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to implement code changes and run tests. This is expected for a coding workflow, but users should apply it only in trusted repositories and review generated changes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly tells the assistant to change code and run tests. This is legitimate for TDD work but still creates normal filesystem and command-execution exposure.

content.user_title: Implement Minimal TDD Green Phase Code
content.value_statement: Teams often overbuild when moving from a failing test to a passing test. This skill keeps Claude, Codex, or Claude Code focused on the smallest safe implementation that makes tests green.
actual_capabilities: Guides the assistant to inspect failing tests before writing implementation code.
Promotes the smallest code change needed to pass the next test.
Provides green phase patterns for JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Django, React, and Express.
Encourages test execution after each change to confirm progress.
Tracks shortcuts and technical debt for a later refactor phase.
Helps avoid adding features that are not required by current tests.
limitations: It does not design a full architecture or production hardening plan.
It depends on existing failing tests that clearly express the desired behavior.
It may create intentionally simple code that needs later refactoring.
It cannot verify business requirements that are not represented by tests.

use_cases.title: Turn a failing unit test green
use_cases.description: Use the skill after writing a failing test to implement only the behavior that test requires.

use_cases.title: Keep pair programming focused
use_cases.description: Use the skill to keep AI-assisted work scoped to the next test instead of broad redesign.

use_cases.title: Teach disciplined TDD flow
use_cases.description: Use the examples to show teams how fake, obvious, and triangulated implementations evolve.

prompt_templates.title: Pass the next failing test
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill on the current failing test. Identify the smallest implementation change, make it, run the relevant test, and report any shortcut to revisit later.

prompt_templates.title: Avoid overbuilding a feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to implement only what the failing tests require. Do not add validation, persistence, or abstractions unless the tests force them.

prompt_templates.title: Green a mixed test suite safely
prompt_templates.scenario: Several tests are failing after a new behavior was added
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to inspect the failing tests, choose the smallest next fix, update the code, run targeted tests, then run broader tests if the targeted suite passes.

prompt_templates.title: Document green phase debt
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to make the tests pass with minimal code, then produce a short refactor handoff listing simplifications, fake implementations, and missing hardening.

output_examples.input: A user creation test fails because the service does not return an identifier.
output_examples.output: The assistant adds the smallest identifier behavior, runs the focused test, confirms it passes, and notes that persistence remains deferred.

output_examples.input: A React component test expects a name and email to render.
output_examples.output: The assistant renders the required fields with simple markup, avoids adding styling or editing controls, and records those as refactor topics.

output_examples.input: An API endpoint test expects a created task response.
output_examples.output: The assistant returns the required response shape, verifies the endpoint test, and avoids introducing a service layer until tests require it.

best_practices: Start with the single failing test that gives the clearest next requirement.
Run the smallest relevant test command after each implementation change.
Write down fake behavior and shortcuts so the refactor phase has clear targets.
anti_patterns: Adding validation, caching, or architecture before a test requires it.
Changing tests just to make the suite pass.
Treating green phase code as finished production design without refactoring.

faq.question: What is the TDD green phase?
faq.answer: It is the step where you write just enough implementation code to make a failing test pass.

faq.question: Does this skill write new tests?
faq.answer: No. It assumes the red phase already produced failing tests and focuses on passing them.

faq.question: Can it be used with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow is written for AI coding assistants that can inspect code, edit files, and run tests.

faq.question: Will the generated code be production ready?
faq.answer: Not always. The skill favors minimal passing code and expects a later refactor phase.

faq.question: What should I provide before using it?
faq.answer: Provide the failing tests, relevant error output, and the target files if the assistant cannot infer them.

faq.question: How should I review the result?
faq.answer: Check that tests pass, no unrelated behavior changed, and any shortcuts are documented for refactoring.
